nativeRange.habitat: Blackwater and whitewater floodplain rivers; slow-moving or still waters with surface vegetation

diet.notes: Large predator; in captivity tends to accept large floating pellets, frozen krill, silversides, whole shrimp, mussels, chopped fish flesh, crickets, and earthworms. Feeder fish are generally discouraged due to disease risk and nutritional imbalance. Mammalian or avian meat should be avoided. Feeds primarily from the surface; superior mouth position facilitates upward prey capture.

summary: Iconic Amazonian 'living fossil' that can reach 90cm and is known to leap up to 2m clear of the water — strictly for dedicated keepers with custom-built 2000L+ setups.
careNotes:
Silver Arowana is generally considered unsuitable for the typical home aquarium; most juveniles sold in the trade (~10cm at purchase) fail to reach maturity in private collections due to inadequate tank size. Growth rate is rapid — roughly 30cm per year in the first two years — so early planning for an adult-sized setup is essential.

Escape risk is extreme: these fish are capable of jumping over 2m clear of the water surface to catch prey and will readily jump if startled or if the tank lid has any gap. A heavy, tight-fitting cover with no openings is non-negotiable.

Adult specimens are highly territorial toward conspecifics and will consume any fish small enough to fit in their mouth (generally anything under ~30cm). Community setups are possible only in very large tanks with suitably large, non-aggressive tankmates such as large catfish, Cichla cichlids, or pacus.

Drop eye syndrome is commonly reported when arowanas are kept in tanks where they habitually look downward for food; feeding from the surface and avoiding bottom-only feeding helps reduce this risk. Tank decor should avoid sharp edges.

Asian Arowana (Scleropages formosus) is often confused with Silver Arowana but is a distinct species listed under CITES Appendix I and is prohibited in many countries including the USA. Silver Arowana (Osteoglossum bicirrhosum) is legal to keep but requires enormous custom infrastructure.

Water quality should be maintained with weekly large water changes (30–50%); high dissolved oxygen and moderate flow are beneficial. The species tolerates a wide pH range (5.0–7.5) and is found in both blackwater and whitewater habitats in the wild.

gH disagreement across sources: SeriouslyFish reported 36–268 ppm (≈2–15 dGH); Fishkeeping World reported 1–8 dGH. Averaged to 2–12 dGH for this entry.
breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder; males carry eggs, larvae, and early juveniles in the mouth for approximately six weeks. Breeding in home aquaria has not been reliably documented; commercial production occurs in large outdoor ponds in Southeast Asia and South America. Spawning in the wild is seasonal (flood season, approximately December–January). Typical brood size is 12–30 fry, which measure 50–75mm at the time of release.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source used in place of Aquarium Co-Op (no species page found for Silver Arowana on that site after multiple URL attempts and site search).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name, family, order, max size (90cm TL), and native range. Temperature stated as 24–30°C, aligning with SeriouslyFish upper range.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters, size, lifespan (10–15 years captive), tank size (300 gal / ~1136L — biased to 2000L per playbook large-predator rule), and care level. gH reported as 1–8 dGH vs SeriouslyFish 2–15 dGH; averaged to 2–12 dGH. Temperature 24–29°C consistent with consensus range used.

fish.breedingNotes: No confirmed home-aquarium breeding; commercial breeding occurs in large outdoor facilities. Paternal mouthbrooder with brood of 12–30 fry.
